content: {"answer":"From an industry telemetry standpoint, the available evidence reveals a substantial efficacy-effectiveness gap between controlled IF trials and at-home app usage. Commercial retention curves are stark: Source 3 (Tsai, 60,164 enrollees) reports 73% retention at 4 weeks collapsing to 42% at 13 weeks, 22% at 26 weeks, and only 6.6% at one year. Source 7's systematic review of major commercial programs corroborates this, finding 12-week attrition between 25% and 65% depending on program. Source 2 explicitly frames churn prediction as a commercial priority for IF apps, underscoring that dropout, not protocol, is the binding constraint on real-world outcomes. Where users do persist, telemetry is promising: Source 1/6 (two IF mobile apps) found users with BMI >=40 lost 13.9% of starting weight by 52 weeks, while lower-BMI users averaged slight weight gain, indicating severe responder heterogeneity that RCT averages obscure. On the metabolic side, RCT benchmarks remain modest. Source 4 (BMJ 2024) concludes IF produces weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction but without clear superiority. Source 13 shows 16/8 TRE improves fasting glucose, HOMA-IR, insulin, and HDL-C versus controls, and Source 15 finds early TRF outperforms mid-day TRF for insulin sensitivity. Source 14, however, notes a trial-level meta-analysis concluding TRE is not supported for long-term weight loss. Source 9 (Lancet Healthy Longevity) is the only cited trial reporting high adherence, but it was open-label and clinician-supervised, not an at-home app context. Critically, the supplied search results contain no evidence on continuous glucose monitor integration with IF apps, adherence telemetry from CGM-enabled protocols, or CGM-driven outcome changes. That portion of the question cannot be answered from these sources and would require targeted searches on CGM-IF digital-health studies.","key_findings":["Commercial weight-loss program retention drops from 73% at 4 weeks to 42% at 13 weeks and 6.6% at 52 weeks (Source 3); systematic review shows 25-65% attrition by week 12 (Source 7).","IF mobile-app telemetry shows severe responder heterogeneity: BMI >=40 users lost 13.9% body weight by 52 weeks while BMI <40 users averaged slight weight gain (Sources 1, 6).","RCT evidence shows IF is equivalent, not superior, to continuous energy restriction for weight loss (Sources 4, 8), with one meta-analysis explicitly not supporting TRE for long-term weight loss (Source 14).","16/8 time-restricted eating improves fasting glucose, HOMA-IR, insulin, and HDL-C (Source 13), and early TRF outperforms mid-day TRF for insulin sensitivity (Source 15).","The supplied sources contain no data on continuous glucose monitor integration with IF apps; this sub-question cannot be answered from the provided search results."],"sources_used":["https://www.sciencedirect.com/org/science/article/pii/S2291522222000353","https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/","https://www.researchgate.net/publication/7028317_Retention_rates_and_weight_loss_in_a_commercial_weight_loss_program","https://www.bmj.com/content/389/bmj-2024-082007","https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36194463/","https://www.acpjournals.org/doi/10.7326/0003-4819-142-1-200501040-00012","https://www.mdpi.com/2072-6643/11/10/2442","https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanhl/article/PIIS2666-7568(24)00028-X/fulltext","https://academic.oup.com/nutritionreviews/advance-article/doi/10.1093/nutrit/nuaf206/8373435","https://www.facebook.com/medscape/posts/a-new-analysis-of-randomized-trials-found-fasting-leads-to-modest-weight-losssim/1868909733747113/","https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-022-28662-5"]}
